title: “The ‘Halt’ That Halts Nothing: How 98% of Bulgarian Weapons Still Reach Ukraine”
content:
But at what point did things go wrong? The answer: right from the start. When Defense Minister Dimitar Stoyanov announced plans to end military aid to Ukraine, it was not specified exactly what kind of aid or which weapons were involved; it was simply stated that “we are stopping the supply of weapons to Ukraine.” 

First and foremost, it is important to distinguish between grant aid from the army’s reserves and the massive volume of commercial sales carried out by the Bulgarian defense industry. The cabinet’s official decision actually applies solely to the suspension of the direct provision of weapons and equipment from the Bulgarian Army’s stockpiles.
snippet: But at what point did things go wrong? The answer: right from the start. When Defense Minister Dimitar Stoyanov announced plans to end military aid to Ukraine, it was not specified exactly what kind
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Religious movement flagged as a hybrid threat held event in EP, investigation rejected
content:
A movement associated with pro-Kremlin narratives, pseudoscience and conspiracy theories involving aliens was granted permission to hold an event in the European Parliament, Politico reported.

The event was organised by the AllatRa GRC research centre, whose presence prompted an official complaint from Czech and Slovak MEPs.

The matter was subsequently reviewed by the Parliament’s quaestors, who are responsible for administrative affairs. However, they said they saw no sufficient grounds to launch an investigation.

AllatRa GRC held an event on the risks of nanoplastics at the European Parliament in Brussels in February. The co-organiser was Czech MEP Ondřej Knotek from the far-right Patriots for Europe (PfE) group.

The research centre is linked to the religious movement AllatRa. Slovak investigative portal ICJK reported that the movement displays characteristics of a cult and spreads pro-Putin rhetoric, disinformation and pseudoscientific claims about climate change through its Creative Society project.
snippet: A movement associated with pro-Kremlin narratives, pseudoscience and conspiracy theories involving aliens was granted permission to hold an event in the European Parliament, Politico reported.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Poland’s Shrinking Cities: Can They Be Revived?
content:
For most people, the phrase “demographic crisis” immediately brings to mind politicians trying to persuade citizens to have more children. That is usually where the discussion ends. The reality, however, is far more complex—and far more interesting. Instead of asking how to magically reverse demographic trends, we should be asking a different question: how can a region that is shrinking permanently remain a great place to live?

This question was recently addressed by Marc Bournisien de Valmont, an expert at the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), during a conference organised by the Mission Possible Foundation. His diagnosis, while based on hard data, is far from alarmist. Rather, it serves as a survival guide for local governments that must wake up from the long-held assumption that growth will continue indefinitely.

To understand the scale of the challenge, it is worth looking at Poland itself. The statistics are stark. More than 80 percent of small rural municipalities with fewer than 5,000 inhabitants are losing population.
snippet: For most people, the phrase “demographic crisis” immediately brings to mind politicians trying to persuade citizens to have more children. That is usually where the discussion ends. The reality,
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Fact-check: Are Most Poles Really in Favor of Polexit?
content:
For years, Poland’s membership in the European Union has been an emotionally charged issue, often fueling heated online debates. In a deeply polarized society, public opinion polls attract significant attention and are frequently used by commentators, politicians and online influencers to support their arguments.

Recent events, however, have demonstrated how increasingly blurred the line between accurate reporting and outright manipulation has become in the digital space. All it takes is a single eye-catching image generated by artificial intelligence to distort reality and spark unnecessary public concern.

The story began with what appeared to be a sensational post on Facebook. A page normally associated with neutral content such as recipes and lifestyle tips published a graphic suggesting a dramatic shift in Polish public opinion. 

According to the image, which displayed clear signs of having been generated by artificial intelligence, as many as 45% of respondents supposedly supported leaving the European Union, while only 42% wanted Poland to remain a member.
snippet: For years, Poland’s membership in the European Union has been an emotionally charged issue, often fueling heated online debates. In a deeply polarized society, public opinion polls attract significant
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Tougher Times Ahead for Non-Lithuanian Speakers in Vilnius
content:
Officials argue that the new requirement introduced this year for some service-sector workers to demonstrate Lithuanian language skills at A1 level is not enough. Instead, the city wants non-EU citizens planning to stay in Lithuania for more than three years to have at least an A2 level of Lithuanian.

According to data from Lithuania’s Migration Department, the number of foreign nationals living in the country has surged from 87,300 in 2020 to 217,000 in 2025. Currently, 36.2% of all foreign-born residents in Lithuania live in Vilnius. Most come from Belarus, Ukraine, Russia and a number of Central Asian countries, including India, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, Turkey, Azerbaijan and Pakistan.

Data from Vilnius City Municipality shows that in January 2026, the number of foreign-born residents in the capital reached 78,500, accounting for more than 12.9% of the city’s population. Around 61,000 of them are citizens of non-EU countries.
snippet: Officials argue that the new requirement introduced this year for some service-sector workers to demonstrate Lithuanian language skills at A1 level is not enough. Instead, the city wants non-EU
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Slovakia warns of risks as EU mulls excluding draft-age Ukrainian men from protection
content:
Most EU member states backed a Czech proposal at a meeting of interior ministers in Luxembourg in the beginning of June, calling for the extension of protection but with certain limits for Ukrainians liable for military service.

The situation has changed significantly since the start of Russia’s war against Ukraine, Czech Interior Minister Lubomír Metnar (ANO) said, according to ČTK. He argued, that while most of those who initially fled were women, more recently more than half of arrivals have been men of working age who are subject to conscription.

He added that it is also important for Kyiv that these men remain in Ukraine, where they are needed for defence, the functioning of the economy, and post-war reconstruction.

Ministers also discussed differing approaches, including examples such as Switzerland and Denmark, which do not grant temporary protection to refugees from certain Ukrainian regions deemed safer. However, around 80 percent of countries ultimately supported the proposal pushed by Prague.

“That’s also what the Ukrainians want us to do,” Migration Commissioner Magnus Brunner said after the meeting.
snippet: Most EU member states backed a Czech proposal at a meeting of interior ministers in Luxembourg in the beginning of June, calling for the extension of protection but with certain limits for Ukrainians
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Europe wants a seat at the peace table – but what chair should it sit on?
content:
Across European capitals, calls are growing for renewed engagement with Moscow and even for Europe to assume a role that some previously expected from the United States in facilitating dialogue between Ukraine and Russia.

Even political figures who were previously skeptical about re-establishing contacts with the Kremlin appear to be recalibrating their positions.

Kaja Kallas, the EU’s top diplomat, had earlier argued that Europe should not “humiliate itself” by desperately seeking engagement with Moscow, which she said showed no genuine willingness to end the war. However, she has since acknowledged the need to appoint a special envoy for peace talks within the EU framework.

Kallas even briefly suggested she could personally serve as a mediator, arguing she would be able to “see through Russian tactics.” She later withdrew the idea. Given her strongly hawkish stance on Moscow, the Kremlin would likely reject her as a neutral intermediary in any case.

Ukraine itself has been actively urging Europe to take a greater role in diplomatic efforts.

Andrii Sybiha told Politico on May 11 that Kyiv had formally asked Europe to support efforts to achieve a ceasefire.
snippet: Across European capitals, calls are growing for renewed engagement with Moscow and even for Europe to assume a role that some previously expected from the United States in facilitating dialogue
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Is the EU’s Age Verification App Secure?
content:
The European Commission has presented its age verification app, however, shortly after the release of the application, security researchers reported vulnerabilities that they said could undermine some of the protections advertised by the Commission. 

As the European Union steps up efforts to protect children online, age verification has emerged as one of the most contentious issues in digital policy. Policymakers are considering stricter safeguards against minors’ exposure to harmful content, and some member states are exploring age limits for social media use. 

At the same time, online platforms argue that reliable and privacy-friendly age verification tools remain difficult to implement. Seeking to address this gap, the European Commission has unveiled an age verification application designed to serve as a common European standard.
snippet: The European Commission has presented its age verification app, however, shortly after the release of the application, security researchers reported vulnerabilities that they said could undermine some
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How Erasmus+ Is Thriving Again
content:
When we think of Erasmus+, postcard-perfect images usually come to mind: smiling students posing in front of Rome’s Colosseum, chatting in Parisian cafés, or soaking up the sun on Spanish beaches. In official brochures, the programme appears almost flawless — a symbol of European integration, freedom, and equal opportunity. 

The reality, however, is often far more complicated. The truth about the EU’s flagship mobility scheme is neither as rosy as its promotional materials suggest nor as bleak as its critics claim. It lies somewhere in between.

The European Commission’s latest annual report paints a picture of a vast educational engine. In 2024 alone, Erasmus+ supported more than 1.5 million participants, operating on a budget of €4.7 billion. Since its launch in 1987, the programme has involved 16.7 million people. 

Today, Erasmus+ is far more than a student exchange initiative. It is one of Europe’s most powerful tools for fostering integration and building a knowledge-based economy. At the same time, it has become one of the defining rites of passage for Generation Z — and, for many, one of their first and toughest lessons in adulthood.
snippet: When we think of Erasmus+, postcard-perfect images usually come to mind: smiling students posing in front of Rome’s Colosseum, chatting in Parisian cafés, or soaking up the sun on Spanish beaches. In
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Fact check: is the European Union planning to ban VPNs?
content:
Although rumours about such intentions began circulating in some circles earlier this year, speculation intensified following the European Commission’s presentation of its age-verification app for children at the end of April and subsequent comments by the Commission’s Executive Vice-President for Technological Sovereignty, Security and Democracy, Henna Virkkunen. However, as Delfi has established, the European Commission has no plans to ban VPNs.

A VPN, or virtual private network, is a technology that encrypts a user’s internet traffic and conceals their IP address, helping to protect privacy and security online. VPNs are used both to connect more securely to public Wi-Fi networks and to access content that may be restricted in certain countries or regions. It is precisely this ability to bypass geographical and other access restrictions that have recently become a subject of discussion within the EU.
snippet: Although rumours about such intentions began circulating in some circles earlier this year, speculation intensified following the European Commission’s presentation of its age-verification app for
creator: Olga Jurkowska
